summary: O'Neil skewers the Draper University of Heroes, a $9,500 eight-week Silicon Valley entrepreneurship program that she compares to pickup artist training. The curriculum includes pledging allegiance to personal brands, minimal coding instruction, pool time, go-kart racing, and graduation ceremonies involving superhero capes and trampolines. She argues the program is fundamentally a confidence-boosting ritual designed to help young men convince themselves their narcissistic worldview is honorable. The key difference from pickup artistry is that instead of tricking drunk women for hours, these would-be entrepreneurs must trick investors for much longer.
key_points: The program's curriculum prioritizes confidence-building theater over substantive skills, with only two days of coding amid weeks of networking and leisure activities
The gender imbalance is not accidental but reflects the program's design as a male confidence ritual similar to pickup artist communities
Both pickup artistry and Silicon Valley hustle culture share a core structure: convincing participants their narcissistic goals are noble and just require the right tricks
The reading list (Trump, Ayn Rand, Wall Street MBA) reveals the underlying ideology of self-serving individualism dressed up as heroism
money_quotes: Even just the name of the school – the Draper University of Heroes – feels like an Onion article
They pledge allegiance every morning to their personal brands
They submit to a full two days of coding and excel lessons
Tesla (the new conspicuous consumption choice among pseudo-progressive capitalists)
It's fundamentally a confidence-boosting ritual, where a bunch of guys convince themselves that their prospects are good, their goals are attainable, their narcissistic world view is honorable
It's just a question of acquiring the right magic tricks to entrap their prey
Whereas the pick up artists only needed to trick drunk women for a few hours in order to sleep with them, these 'Silicon Valley Heroes' have to trick way more people for way longer
key_insight: Silicon Valley entrepreneurship culture operates on the same psychological mechanics as pickup artistry: confidence rituals that dress up manipulation and narcissism as heroic self-actualization.
